theory.intro: Rectangles with the same area can have different side lengths, and rectangles with the same perimeter can also have different shapes.
facts: Area $=$ length $\times$ width; many length/width pairs give the **same area**.
Perimeter $=$ $2 \times (\text{length} + \text{width})$; many pairs give the **same perimeter**.
Same area does **not** mean same perimeter, and the reverse is also true.
steps: List length/width pairs that multiply to the target area (or add to half the perimeter).
Compute the other measure (perimeter or area) for each pair.
Compare to see how the shape changes.

foundation.q: Find another whole-number rectangle with the same area as this one.
A rectangle is $8$ cm by $3$ cm. Give another with the same area.
Two rectangles each have area $20\text{ cm}^2$. One is $4 \times 5$. Give whole dimensions for the other.
Find another rectangle with the same perimeter as a $5$ cm by $3$ cm rectangle.
List all whole-number rectangles with area $6\text{ cm}^2$.
A rectangle is $2$ cm by $9$ cm. Give a rectangle with the same area but different shape.
Find the area of this rectangle, then name another with the same area.
List all whole-number rectangles with area $12\text{ cm}^2$.
A rectangle is $7$ cm by $1$ cm. Give another with the same perimeter.
Do a $2 \times 6$ and a $3 \times 4$ rectangle have the same area?

development.q: A $2 \times 8$ and a $4 \times 4$ rectangle both have area $16\text{ cm}^2$. Find each perimeter and compare.
development.a: $20$ cm vs $16$ cm — same area, different perimeter

development.q: List the perimeters of all whole-number rectangles with area $24\text{ cm}^2$.
Which rectangle with area $36\text{ cm}^2$ has the smallest perimeter?
Two gardens have perimeter $24$ m. One is $8 \times 4$. Find the area of a $6 \times 6$ garden and compare.
development.a: $32\text{ m}^2$ vs $36\text{ m}^2$ — same perimeter, different area

development.q: A rectangle has area $18\text{ cm}^2$ and width $3$ cm. A second has the same area but width $2$ cm. Find both lengths.
Of all rectangles with perimeter $20$ cm, which has the largest area?
Find the missing dimension so this rectangle has the same area as a $6 \times 6$ square.
A $1 \times 16$ and a $4 \times 4$ rectangle both have area $16\text{ cm}^2$. Which uses more fencing (perimeter)?
List two different rectangles, each with perimeter $18$ cm, and give their areas.
A rectangle measures $10$ m by $2$ m. Reshape it (whole numbers) to the same area with the smallest perimeter.

mastery.q: A farmer has $36$ m of fencing. Find the rectangular paddock dimensions giving the largest area, and state that area.
Two rectangles have area $48\text{ cm}^2$. One is a square's near-twin $6 \times 8$; the other is $3 \times 16$. Find the difference in their perimeters.
A rectangle has perimeter $40$ cm. Express its area when the length is $15$ cm, then when it is $12$ cm.
All rectangles with area $100\text{ cm}^2$ and whole sides: which has the largest perimeter?
Two paddocks have the same perimeter $60$ m. One is square, one is $20 \times 10$. Find the area each and the difference.
mastery.a: Square $225\text{ m}^2$, other $200\text{ m}^2$; difference $25\text{ m}^2$

mastery.q: A rectangle's area is $60\text{ cm}^2$. As whole sides move from $6 \times 10$ toward $1 \times 60$, what happens to the perimeter?
Find dimensions of a rectangle with area $64\text{ cm}^2$ and the smallest possible whole-number perimeter.
A rectangle $12 \times 3$ is reshaped to keep the same area but be a square. Is that possible with whole sides? Explain.
mastery.a: No — area is $36$, and $\sqrt{36} = 6$, so $6 \times 6$ works (it is possible)

mastery.q: Two rectangles share both the same area ($24\text{ cm}^2$) and same perimeter ($20$ cm). Find their dimensions.
A garden bed is $9$ m by $4$ m. Reshape to the same area as whole sides giving the smallest perimeter, and state how much fencing is saved.
